does: This paper introduces the Right-to-Act protocol, a deterministic pre-execution decision layer that sits between AI system outputs and real-world action execution. Unlike existing post-hoc validation approaches, this framework operates prospectively to evaluate whether an AI-generated decision satisfies necessary conditions before implementation. The core mechanism enforces non-compensatory decision logic: if any required structural condition fails, execution is halted or deferred regardless of confidence scores or other high-performing signals. The authors formally distinguish compensatory systems (where strengths can offset weaknesses) from non-compensatory regimes and define a pre-execution legitimacy boundary that establishes minimum threshold requirements across multiple dimensions. Case studies demonstrate how identical AI outputs produce different execution outcomes when filtered through this protocol, highlighting the practical implications of gating mechanism design.
connects: This work extends the safety engineering tradition of using hard constraints and formal verification methods to AI deployment contexts. It builds on prior research in AI governance and risk management that recognizes limitations of purely probabilistic and post-hoc approaches, responding to growing recognition that model accuracy and confidence alone cannot guarantee safe real-world deployment. The framework draws inspiration from high-stakes domains like aviation and nuclear engineering where non-compensatory requirements prevent failure propagation. It connects to literature on decision support systems, permission-based architectures, and recent work on AI system oversight that emphasizes structural safeguards over statistical guarantees. The pre-execution layer concept engages with emerging discussions about human-AI collaboration boundaries and the need for explicit authorization mechanisms before consequential actions.
enables: This protocol establishes a foundation for designing AI systems with explicit legitimacy gates, enabling safer deployment in high-stakes domains including medical decision-support, autonomous vehicles, and financial systems. The framework opens avenues for developing formal specifications of non-negotiable preconditions and techniques for dynamically evaluating them in real-time. Future work could extend this toward hierarchical governance architectures combining multiple pre-execution layers and developing methods to identify which conditions should remain non-compensatory versus compensatory for specific domains. The approach supports building more transparent AI pipelines where human operators understand precisely what combinations of conditions prevent execution. It also enables comparative governance research examining how different industries operationalize legitimacy boundaries and points toward standardization efforts for pre-execution protocols across safety-critical AI applications.
